content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 4 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Hebrew Base:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 200–202
**LXX Reference:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 146–147
**Disagreement Log:** `disagreements/DEU_004.md`

---

## Council Notes

Chapter 4 is the theological heart of Moses' first address: the radical aniconism of Sinai (vv.1–20), the warning of exile and return (vv.25–31), the cosmic argument for the LORD's uniqueness (vv.32–39), and the closing geographical frame with the cities of refuge (vv.41–49). It contains some of the most theologically dense language in the book.

Key rulings: "ten words" for *aseret ha-devarim* (not "Ten Commandments" — the Hebrew term is *devarim*); "statutes and ordinances" (consistent Deuteronomic terminology); "iron furnace" (not softened); "El qanna" rendered as "a jealous God" (not softened); "consuming fire" kept; "with all your heart and with all your soul" (Shema-language preserved identically); "a merciful God" (*El rachum*) over "compassionate"; "the latter days" (*acharit ha-yamim*) over "final days"; "with his own presence" (*be-fanav*) not softened to "power."

The LXX at v.48 reads *Siryon* where the MT reads *Sion* — a textual variant. SOPHIA follows the MT.

---

## Consensus Translation

1 "And now, O Israel, hear the statutes and the ordinances that I am teaching you to do, so that you may live and go in and possess the land that the LORD, the God of your fathers, is giving to you.

2 You shall not add to the word that I am commanding you, and you shall not take away from it — so that you may keep the commandments of the LORD your God that I am commanding you.

3 Your eyes have seen what the LORD did at Baal-peor, for every man who went after Baal of Peor — the LORD your God destroyed him from among you.

4 But you who held fast to the LORD your God are all alive today.

5 See — I have taught you statutes and ordinances as the LORD my God commanded me, so that you should do them in the midst of the land into which you are entering to possess it.

6 Keep them and do them, for that is your wisdom and your understanding in the sight of the peoples, who shall hear all these statutes and shall say: 'Surely this great nation is a wise and understanding people.'

7 For what great nation is there that has a god so near to it as the LORD our God is to us whenever we call to him?

8 And what great nation is there that has statutes and ordinances as righteous as all this instruction that I am setting before you today?

9 Only take care and keep your soul diligently, lest you forget the things that your eyes have seen, and lest they depart from your heart all the days of your life. Make them known to your children and your children's children —

10 the day that you stood before the LORD your God at Horeb, when the LORD said to me: 'Gather the people to me, so that I may let them hear my words, that they may learn to fear me all the days that they live on the earth, and that they may teach their children so.'

11 And you drew near and stood at the foot of the mountain, while the mountain burned with fire to the heart of the heavens — darkness, cloud, and thick darkness.

12 And the LORD spoke to you from the midst of the fire. A voice of words you heard, but you saw no form — only a voice.

13 And he declared to you his covenant, which he commanded you to do — the Ten Words — and he wrote them on two tablets of stone.

14 And the LORD commanded me at that time to teach you statutes and ordinances, so that you might do them in the land to which you are crossing over to possess.

15 So keep your souls diligently — for you saw no form on the day the LORD spoke to you at Horeb from the midst of the fire —

16 lest you act corruptly and make for yourselves a carved image in the form of any figure: the likeness of male or female,

17 the likeness of any animal that is on the earth, the likeness of any winged bird that flies in the heavens,

18 the likeness of any thing that creeps on the ground, the likeness of any fish that is in the waters below the earth.

19 And lest you lift up your eyes toward the heavens and see the sun and the moon and the stars — all the host of the heavens — and be led astray and bow down to them and serve them, which the LORD your God has allotted to all the peoples under all the heavens.

20 But the LORD took you and brought you out of the iron furnace, out of Egypt, to be a people of his own inheritance, as at this day.

21 And the LORD was angry with me on your account and swore that I would not cross the Jordan and that I would not enter the good land that the LORD your God is giving you as an inheritance.

22 For I am to die in this land — I shall not cross the Jordan — but you are to cross and take possession of that good land.

23 Take care lest you forget the covenant of the LORD your God that he made with you, and make for yourselves a carved image in the form of anything that the LORD your God has forbidden you.

24 For the LORD your God is a consuming fire, a jealous God.

25 When you father children and children's children, and have grown old in the land, and act corruptly by making a carved image in the form of anything — doing what is evil in the sight of the LORD your God so as to provoke him to anger —

26 I call heaven and earth to witness against you today, that you will soon utterly perish from the land to which you are crossing the Jordan to possess. You will not prolong your days in it, but will be utterly destroyed.

27 And the LORD will scatter you among the peoples, and you will be left few in number among the nations where the LORD will drive you.

28 And there you will serve gods, the work of human hands — wood and stone — which neither see nor hear nor eat nor smell.

29 But from there you will seek the LORD your God, and you will find him, when you search for him with all your heart and with all your soul.

30 When you are in distress and all these things overtake you in the latter days, you will return to the LORD your God and obey his voice.

31 For the LORD your God is a merciful God. He will not abandon you or destroy you or forget the covenant with your fathers that he swore to them.

32 For ask now of the days past that were before you, from the day that God created man on the earth, and from one end of the heavens to the other — whether there has been anything like this great thing, or whether anything like it has been heard of.

33 Has any people heard the voice of God speaking from the midst of fire, as you have heard, and lived?

34 Or has God tried to come and take for himself a nation from within a nation, by trials, by signs, by wonders, and by war, by a strong hand and an outstretched arm, and by great terrors — according to all that the LORD your God did for you in Egypt before your eyes?

35 To you it was shown so that you might know that the LORD — he is God; there is no other besides him.

36 From the heavens he let you hear his voice to discipline you. And on the earth he showed you his great fire, and his words you heard from the midst of the fire.

37 And because he loved your fathers and chose their offspring after them, he brought you out with his own presence, by his great power, from Egypt,

38 to drive out before you nations greater and mightier than you, to bring you in, to give you their land as an inheritance, as at this day.

39 Know therefore today, and take it to your heart, that the LORD — he is God in the heavens above and on the earth below; there is no other.

40 Therefore keep his statutes and his commandments that I am commanding you today, so that it may go well with you and with your children after you, and that you may prolong your days in the land that the LORD your God is giving you for all time.

41 Then Moses set apart three cities beyond the Jordan toward the sunrise,

42 for the manslayer to flee there — one who kills his neighbor unintentionally, without having been at enmity with him in time past — and that by fleeing to one of these cities he might live:

43 Bezer in the wilderness in the tableland for the Reubenites, and Ramoth in Gilead for the Gadites, and Golan in Bashan for the Manassehites.

44 This is the instruction that Moses set before the sons of Israel.

45 These are the testimonies and the statutes and the ordinances that Moses spoke to the sons of Israel when they came out of Egypt —

46 beyond the Jordan in the valley opposite Beth-peor, in the land of Sihon king of the Amorites, who dwelt at Heshbon, whom Moses and the sons of Israel struck down when they came out of Egypt.

47 And they took possession of his land and the land of Og king of Bashan — the two Amorite kings who were beyond the Jordan toward the sunrise —

48 from Aroer, which is on the edge of the Wadi Arnon, as far as Mount Sion — that is, Hermon —

49 and all the Arabah beyond the Jordan to the east, as far as the sea of the Arabah, under the slopes of Pisgah.

---

## Verse-by-Verse Ruling Notes

**V.1 — "O Israel" / "ordinances" vs. "judgments":** EMET: "ordinances." LOGOS: "judgments." PNEUMA: "ordinances." SOPHIA: "ordinances" — *mishpatim* in Deuteronomic context is better rendered "ordinances" (the standard legal sense) than "judgments" (which implies judicial rulings). This convention is maintained throughout.

**V.4 — "held fast" vs. "cleave":** EMET: "you who cleave to the LORD." LOGOS: "you who held fast to the LORD." PNEUMA: "all of you who held fast to the LORD." The Hebrew *ha-doveqim* (Qal participle of *davaq* — to cling, cleave, hold fast) — both "held fast" and "cleave" are accurate. SOPHIA: "held fast" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — more natural English.

**V.8 — "Torah" vs. "instruction":** EMET/LOGOS: "this Torah." PNEUMA: "this instruction." Per project convention, "instruction" for *torah* in running text. SOPHIA adopts "instruction."

**V.11 — "thick darkness" vs. "thick gloom":** EMET/LOGOS: "thick darkness." PNEUMA: "thick gloom." Hebrew *arafel* = heavy storm-cloud, thick darkness. SOPHIA: "thick darkness" (EMET/LOGOS) — both capture the term; "darkness" is the more established rendering.

**V.13 — "Ten Words" vs. "Ten Commandments":** EMET/LOGOS: "the Ten Words." PNEUMA: "the Ten Words." The Hebrew *aseret ha-devarim* = the ten words/things. All three translators agree. SOPHIA: "the Ten Words" — distinguishing from *aseret ha-dibrot* ("ten commandments").

**V.19 — "led astray" vs. "drawn away":** EMET: "be led astray." LOGOS: "be drawn away." PNEUMA: "be drawn away." Hebrew *nidachta* — to be driven away, pushed away. SOPHIA: "be led astray" (EMET) — the passive, subtle quality of idolatrous seduction is better captured by "led astray."

**V.19 — "which the LORD your God has allotted to all the peoples":** EMET: "which the LORD your God has allotted." LOGOS: "things that the LORD your God has allotted." PNEUMA: "The LORD your God has apportioned these to all the peoples." All three preserve this remarkable verse. SOPHIA follows LOGOS.

**V.20 — "iron furnace" vs. "iron-smelting furnace":** EMET/LOGOS: "the iron furnace." PNEUMA: "that iron-smelting furnace." Hebrew *kur ha-barzel* = the iron furnace. SOPHIA: "the iron furnace" (EMET/LOGOS) — plain and accurate; PNEUMA's "smelting" is an addition.

**V.20 — "people of his own inheritance" vs. "people of his own possession":** EMET: "a people of inheritance." LOGOS: "a people of his own possession." PNEUMA: "a people for his very own inheritance." Hebrew *am nahalah* — a people of inheritance/patrimony. SOPHIA: "a people of his own inheritance" — combining the precision of *nahalah* (inheritance) with "his own" for emphasis.

**V.22 — "I shall not cross the Jordan" vs. "I am not crossing":** EMET: "I am not crossing the Yarden." LOGOS: "I shall not cross the Jordan." PNEUMA: "I will not cross the Jordan." SOPHIA: "I shall not cross the Jordan" (LOGOS) — the modal "shall" carries the sense of divinely sealed fate.

**V.29 — "with all your heart and with all your soul" vs. "with all your heart and all your being":** EMET/LOGOS: "with all your heart and with all your soul." PNEUMA: "with all your heart and all your being." The Hebrew *be-khol levavkha u-ve-khol nafshekha* — the Shema language. SOPHIA: "with all your heart and with all your soul" (EMET/LOGOS) — the established Shema vocabulary must be consistent. "Soul" for *nefesh* here, not "being" (reserved for PNEUMA's distinctive choices elsewhere).

**V.30 — "the latter days" vs. "the final days" vs. "in future times":** EMET: "in the end of days." LOGOS: "in the latter days." PNEUMA: "in the final days." Hebrew *be-acharit ha-yamim* — the latter/end of days, a phrase with eschatological overtones. SOPHIA: "the latter days" (LOGOS) — preserves the temporal ambiguity.

**V.31 — "a merciful God" vs. "a compassionate God":** EMET: "a merciful God." LOGOS: "a merciful God." PNEUMA: "a compassionate God." Hebrew *El rachum* — the root *racham* derives from *rechem* (womb), meaning viscerally maternal mercy. SOPHIA: "a merciful God" (EMET/LOGOS) — the established rendering; "compassionate" (PNEUMA) is also theologically accurate but inconsistent with the established convention.

**V.37 — "with his own presence" vs. "himself, by his great power":** EMET: "with his presence, with his great power." LOGOS: "with his own presence, by his great power." PNEUMA: "out of Egypt himself, by his great power." The Hebrew *be-fanav* = "by his face/presence." SOPHIA: "with his own presence, by his great power" (LOGOS) — the LXX softens this to "by his own power"; SOPHIA follows the MT in preserving the anthropomorphic "presence" (*panim*).

**V.48 — "Mount Sion" vs. "Mount Sirion":** EMET: "Mount Siryon." LOGOS: "Mount Sirion." PNEUMA: "Mount Sirion." The MT reads *שִׂיאֹן* (Sion), while the LXX Vaticanus and Samaritan Pentateuch read *Siryon*. SOPHIA follows the MT: "Mount Sion" — noting the variant without adopting the LXX reading.

---

## Textual Notes for the Consensus Record

**V.2 — Canonical boundary formula:** "You shall not add...you shall not take away" — this formula brackets the entire Torah (cf. Deut 12:32; Rev 22:18). It establishes Deuteronomy's self-understanding as a complete and bounded text.

**V.12 — Sinai aniconism:** The theological foundation for the entire anti-idol polemic (vv.15–19): Israel heard but did not see. Therefore no visible form can represent what was never visually disclosed. This is one of the most sophisticated theological arguments in the Pentateuch.

**V.19 — The allotment of the heavenly host:** This verse's claim — that the LORD assigned the sun, moon, and stars to the nations — is a theologically remarkable statement within the MT. It implies a form of cosmic order where each nation has its patrons; Israel's patron is YHWH himself. The LXX Vaticanus preserves *ἀπένειμεν* (apportioned) exactly matching *chalaq*.

**V.48 — Textual variant at Sion/Siryon:** The MT reads *Sion* here; LXX Vaticanus, Samaritan Pentateuch, and Deuteronomy 3:9 identify *Siryon* as Hermon's Amorite name. This is a genuine textual discrepancy within the MT itself. SOPHIA preserves the MT reading while noting the variant.
